sweep.rule: A fact is stated only if >=2 independent sources agree. Pending/forward events are kept in the future tense. Every datapoint is tagged sourced or estimated.

lead: US equities closed Monday at fresh records, the Dow above 53,000 for the first time at 53,056, the S&P 500 at 7,537 and the Nasdaq at 26,121, with the VIX near 15.6. That combination says the tape is priced for a soft landing rather than braced for a shock, which keeps downside protection inexpensive and argues for trimming into strength rather than chasing it.

The mood turned overnight in Asia, and the driver was a chip story rather than a macro one. Samsung reported the largest quarterly operating profit in its history, roughly $58bn, yet the stock fell about 9% as attention shifted to peak-memory worries and the durability of AI capex; SK Hynix fell a further 7% to 9% and the KOSPI closed down about 3.6%. The read is two-sided, because the numbers confirm a genuine memory supercycle even as the market begins to sell the record, and it is that reflex that now caps the near term for memory and AI names, Micron included, where the better entry is more likely to arrive on the fade than on the strength.
full: US equities closed Monday at fresh records, the Dow above 53,000 for the first time at 53,056, the S&P 500 at 7,537 and the Nasdaq at 26,121, with the VIX near 15.6. That combination says the tape is priced for a soft landing rather than braced for a shock, which keeps downside protection inexpensive and argues for trimming into strength rather than chasing it.

The mood turned overnight in Asia, and the driver was a chip story rather than a macro one. Samsung reported the largest quarterly operating profit in its history, roughly $58bn, yet the stock fell about 9% as attention shifted to peak-memory worries and the durability of AI capex; SK Hynix fell a further 7% to 9% and the KOSPI closed down about 3.6%. The read is two-sided, because the numbers confirm a genuine memory supercycle even as the market begins to sell the record, and it is that reflex that now caps the near term for memory and AI names, Micron included, where the better entry is more likely to arrive on the fade than on the strength.

A separate overlay sits alongside the chip move and is best not confused with it. An Iranian missile struck a Qatari LNG carrier in the Strait of Hormuz, the first vessel strike since the interim truce, yet Brent rose only modestly to around $72. The episode re-arms an oil tail the market had largely written off, which is a reason to keep some energy and gold in the book as a hedge, not a reason to trade the equity tape around the headline.

Rates are where the week is decided. June payrolls came in soft at 57,000 against expectations near 115,000, with the prior two months revised down a combined 74,000, and the odds of a September hike have faded to about even from roughly two-thirds. With the ten-year back testing 4.5% and the thirty-year sitting on the 5% handle, the three, ten and thirty-year auctions running through Thursday and the June FOMC minutes on Wednesday, the first under Chair Warsh, become the swing factor; a clean set of auctions and a pause-leaning tone would cap yields and support range-based structures such as the ten-year accrual, while a soft auction would push through 4.5% and pressure long-duration equity.

The hyperscaler complex has begun to mend, Microsoft near $387 and Broadcom near $374 after both bounced off last week's lows, but the AI-capex derate is only starting to mean-revert and stays unconfirmed until the late-July prints, so the move reads as a defined-risk entry rather than an all-clear. Earnings season opens this week with Levi Strauss, PepsiCo and Delta, and the capex guides that follow will be the real tell for Microsoft, Broadcom and Nvidia.

sweep.note: Tuesday 6am-London desk sweep, 7-Jul-2026. The read splits into a record-setting Monday US close and a Tuesday Asia session that sold off on a memory-chip sell-on-news — with a fresh Middle-East shock a SEPARATE overlay, not the driver of the Korean move. MONDAY (US, sourced): the Dow closed above 53,000 for the first time (53,056, +0.29%), the S&P 500 at a record 7,537 (+0.72%) and the Nasdaq at 26,121 (+1.12%) as chips rebounded; VIX 15.57. OVERNIGHT (Asia, sourced): the KOSPI fell -3.64% to ~7,758 (a sell-side sidecar tripped intraday) on a CHIP sell-on-news, not a geopolitical one. Samsung's preliminary Q2 (7-Jul) was the largest quarterly operating profit in tech history — 89.4tn KRW (~$58.4bn), +~1,810% YoY on a memory supercycle (Citi: DRAM +44% QoQ, NAND +53% QoQ) — beating on profit but MISSING on revenue (171tn KRW), yet the stock FELL ~9% intraday on sell-on-news and 'peak-memory / AI-capex-sustainability' fear (Morgan Stanley's 'grim reaper of semiconductors'); SK Hynix fell ~7-9% (its record ~$29bn Nasdaq ADR, ticker SKHY, debuts 10-Jul). Chip-heavy indices led lower — Nikkei ~-2%, KOSPI worst, Shanghai/Hang Seng down less — the signature of an AI/chip rotation, not a broad oil shock; the PBoC set a weaker-than-expected USD/CNY fix (6.8054 vs 6.7838 est). SEPARATELY, an Iranian missile struck the Qatari LNG carrier Al Rekayyat (owner Nakilat) in the Strait of Hormuz early Tuesday — the first vessel strike since the interim truce — re-arming an oil war premium the market had written off (Brent ~$72, WTI ~$69, up only modestly on the day; OPEC+ agreed a third +188K b/d step for August on 5-Jul); Hormuz set the regional risk-off tone but was not the named driver of the Korean chip move. BACKDROP: the June jobs report (2-Jul) was soft — payrolls +57K vs ~115K consensus, April/May revised down a combined 74K, unemployment 4.2% on a falling participation rate — fading September-hike odds to ~50% (from ~64%; July hike ~20%). RATES: 10Y ~4.48%, back testing 4.5%; the 30Y sits right on the 5.00% handle; Fed band 3.50-3.75%; 3/10/30-year auctions run Tue-Thu and the June FOMC minutes (first under Chair Warsh) land Wednesday 9-Jul. FX: DXY eased to ~100.85; EUR/USD ~1.142 (ECB deposit rate 2.25%, hiked 11-Jun); GBP/USD recovered to ~1.339 (BoE 3.75%, next 30-Jul); USD/JPY ~161.8, back under the 162.00 'line' with no intervention yet and the BoJ now at 1.00% (hiked 16-Jun). COMMODITIES: gold ~$4,130, softening intraday but with a fresh Hormuz bid (Goldman trimmed its target to ~$4,900 in June; JPMorgan still ~$6,000). DISLOCATION: the hyperscaler complex has BOUNCED off last week's lows — MSFT ~$387 (~-20% YTD, up ~$14 off the 30-Jun low), AVGO ~$374 (+3.7% Monday, ~-24% off its 2-Jun ATH), NVDA ~$196 (roughly flat YTD); Oppenheimer's MSFT target is $515 (cut from $630) while the Street mean stays ~$565. WEEK AHEAD: Levi Strauss (Wed 8-Jul), PepsiCo (Thu 9-Jul, pre-open), Delta (Fri 10-Jul, pre-open) open Q2 earnings; a USMCA overhang persists after the US declined to renew the pact (now a 10-year annual-review process). Implied moves are options-desk estimates (labelled estimated); consensus figures are flagged where not dual-confirmed.

earnings.tradeStatement: Stay long Micron (MU) but express it with discipline — two weeks after the record FQ3 (rev $41.46B, EPS $25.11, ~$50B Q4 guide, GM ~86%) the stock has round-tripped part of its spike to ~$985 (roughly a fifth off the 25-Jun ATH near $1,213), and the pricing thesis keeps getting louder (The Transcript, 30-Jun: DRAM revenue +343% YoY driven by price, shortages seen through 2028); the HBM/AI-memory thesis is confirmed AND largely priced, so for concentrated holders monetise the level with a zero-cost collar or call-overwrite, and add on the pullbacks the fade is now handing you rather than chasing.
earnings.name: Micron Technology
earnings.headline: Micron's record blow-out is confirmed and priced — and the fade has extended to ~$976, roughly a fifth off its late-June ATH.
earnings.thesis: Micron reported FQ3 (ended 28-May) after the close on 24 June and it was the print of the quarter: record revenue $41.46B (beating ~$35.25B consensus), non-GAAP EPS $25.11 (vs ~$20.28), and a Q4 guide of ~$50B ±$1B (~86% gross margin), with 16 strategic customer agreements (~$22B of deposits/commitments) underpinning it. HBM3E/HBM4 are sold out through calendar 2027 with demand into 2028; HBM4 12-high is ramping ~2x faster than HBM3E. The stock gapped to an all-time high near $1,213 on 25 June (Goldman lifted its target to $1,100, though kept a Neutral rating), then FADED — trading ~$985 by 7 July, roughly a fifth off the peak, as the post-print momentum rolled over and the chip complex whipsawed (Monday's US chip rebound, then a Tuesday Asia memory selloff led by Samsung — which printed the largest quarterly profit in tech history (~$58bn; a broad memory supercycle, Citi has DRAM +44% and NAND +53% QoQ) yet FELL ~9% on sell-on-news and 'peak-memory' fear, the exact record-then-fade dynamic MU has just lived). That parallel cuts both ways for Micron: it CONFIRMS the pricing supercycle — The Transcript's 30-Jun read flags DRAM revenue +343% YoY on PRICE (not volume) and shortages 'everlasting' through 2028, and MU holds ~21% of the HBM market behind SK Hynix's 58% — while the market's sell-the-record reaction in Samsung is the same caution now capping MU. The thesis is confirmed but largely in the price — this is a 'how do I hold a name that just made and gave back a new high' problem, not a 'buy the event'. For concentrated holders, monetise the move with a collar or overwrite; for others, the post-ATH fade is now handing you the dip to add into, not the strength.

earnings.changeMyMind: Guidance or channel checks pointing to 2027 HBM pricing rolling over, or the booked-capacity contracts being renegotiated — the 2027 pricing path, not the quarter just reported, is the thesis.

earnings.tradeStatement: Long Delta Air Lines (DAL) into Q2 before the open on Friday 10 July — a bellwether read on summer-travel demand and premium-cabin resilience into a fuel-aided bar (Street clusters ~$1.44 diluted EPS, range $1.43-1.54, on revenue ~$17.5B) with sharply cheaper jet fuel (WTI ~$68.8, Brent ~$71.9) a direct margin tailwind; express it with a defined-risk structure (cash-secured puts / reverse convertible) rather than naked stock into an event, especially with the shares overbought (RSI ~72) into the print.
earnings.name: Delta Air Lines
earnings.headline: Delta kicks off travel earnings pre-market 10 July — a fuel-aided bar into peak summer, but the stock runs in overbought
earnings.thesis: Delta reports fiscal Q2 2026 before the opening bell on Friday 10 July — the first read on how peak-summer travel demand and premium-cabin/loyalty resilience are holding up. Consensus clusters around ~$1.44 diluted EPS (the Street spread is wide, $1.43-1.54) on revenue ~$17.5B, roughly flat-to-modestly higher YoY. The setup has two supports: sharply softer jet fuel — the IATA jet-fuel benchmark eased from ~$141.6/bbl in June toward ~$116.6 in early July as crude retreated (WTI ~$68.8, Brent ~$71.9, the lowest since late-February after the US-Iran war premium bled out) — is a direct margin tailwind, and Delta's mix keeps tilting toward higher-margin premium and loyalty revenue that is less sensitive to a slowing main cabin. The offset is now two-sided: the stock has rallied into the print and screens overbought (RSI ~72), AND the overnight Strait-of-Hormuz tanker strike (7-Jul) has re-armed the oil-price tail — the single biggest swing factor in the fuel-tailwind leg, so a sustained crude spike into the print would erode the very support this trade leans on. This is off the desk's strategic themes but a genuine dated catalyst for any book with consumer-cyclical or travel exposure, and a bellwether for the broader industrials/consumer read into Q3. Best expressed pre-print with a defined-risk structure — cash-secured puts to get paid to wait for a better entry, or a reverse convertible — rather than chasing an overbought name into an event.
earnings.offThemeWhy: No standing desk theme on airlines/travel — this is a single-name, dated earnings catalyst judged on its own merit, not a strategic house view.
earnings.changeMyMind: A guide-down on softening main-cabin demand or a corporate-travel air-pocket outweighing the fuel tailwind — or a sustained Hormuz-driven crude spike that reverses the cheaper-jet-fuel support before the print — turns the pre-position into a pass.

pillars.note: A fuel-aided bar into peak season with wide EPS dispersion ($1.43-1.54) caps the clean asymmetry, but the cheaper-fuel tailwind and premium mix skew the risk toward 'better than feared'.
Street cautious — EPS guided sharply lower YoY; no clean buy-side alignment ahead of the print.
Dated Q2 print before the open on 10 Jul — a real, scheduled single-name catalyst that also opens travel-earnings season.
Cyclical, sentiment-sensitive name; some caution priced but no clean positioning extreme into the print. RSI 68 — no sentiment extreme. [sourced: TradingView].

exEarnings.headline: Microsoft is ~20% off its high but bouncing off the late-June low — own the AI-capex-derate reversal in a quality compounder with a defined-risk call spread into the July print
exEarnings.thesis: Microsoft was re-rated harder than the franchise warrants — to a ~$373 low on 30-Jun, roughly -23% YTD at the trough — as the market treated the ~$190B of planned 2026 AI infrastructure spend (Q3 capex ~$30.9B, +84% YoY) as a margin drag with an unproven payback rather than the build-out behind Azure/Copilot monetization. It has since bounced ~$14 to ~$387 (~-20% YTD), the derate starting to reverse. The tell throughout is that sell-side conviction never broke with the price — roughly 52 buy / 0 sell ratings and a mean target near $565 (~45%+ upside), even after Oppenheimer cut its target to $515 (from $630) on the capex line — while the multiple compressed to a ~22 P/E near the low end of its multi-year range. Two independent voices frame the setup: SemiAnalysis (6-Jul) argues the build-out is now debt-financed (cumulative AI capex ~$11.1T through 2029; financing, not chips or power, the binding constraint) — i.e. the spend is real and funded, not discretionary cost; and Citrini (24-Jun), while flagging the AI trade as crowded and trimming exposure, expects exactly these positioning-driven 10-15% drawdowns in names still worth owning. The variant view: the market priced capex as pure cost; if the payback window holds, this was a dip in a compounder, not a broken thesis. Because the late-July FQ4 print is genuinely two-sided (a raised capex guide into any Azure deceleration would confirm the fear), express it with DEFINED risk — a 3-6 month call spread, or a buffered note — not naked length.

variant.us: The derate was overdone for a compounder whose sell-side thesis is intact; the bounce off ~$373 is the recovery starting — own it with defined risk into a two-sided print
variant.gap: Market priced ~$190B capex as cost with no payback credit; SemiAnalysis says the spend is debt-financed and real; if monetization holds, the multiple re-rates back up

exEarnings.changeMyMind: A late-July print that pairs a RAISED capex guide with a decelerating Azure — spend rising as monetization stalls — or management conceding the AI payback window is lengthening; that turns the bounce back into a justified re-rating lower.

exEarnings.headline: Broadcom is ~24% off its high but bouncing (+3.7% Monday) while its AI revenue accelerates +143% — the read-through trade is discounted before the read-through happened
exEarnings.thesis: Our own read of the same fault line the MSFT idea trades: the market pre-priced the downstream damage of a hyperscaler capex cut into Broadcom — down to a ~$361 trough against a 52-week high near $495 — while the delivered numbers keep accelerating: revenue ~$22.2B last quarter (+48% YoY), AI semiconductor revenue ~$10.8B (+143% YoY), and a Q3 AI guide of ~$16B (over +200% YoY) on custom-accelerator design wins across Google, Meta and OpenAI. It has since bounced +3.7% to ~$374, the derate starting to unwind. The derate is a probability statement — that hyperscalers will cut the very spend that feeds this backlog — marked as near-certain while MSFT alone still guides ~$190B for 2026 and SemiAnalysis (6-Jul) argues that spend is now debt-financed and structural. If the capex guides hold through the late-July/August prints, AVGO's drawdown keeps reversing off delivered growth; if a major hyperscaler actually cuts >10%, the thesis is wrong and the defined-risk structure caps the damage. That two-sidedness is why this is a call spread and not naked stock. Own view: the numbers and the drawdown are the argument — no outside author's work is load-bearing here.

exEarnings.name: 10Y range accrual

chart.caption: US 10-year Treasury yield, daily — shown against the note's 4.00–4.90% accrual band and the 5% ceiling that has capped every test since Oct-2023.

exEarnings.trigger: The 10Y is back at ~4.49%, retesting the 4.5% area, into Wednesday's June FOMC minutes, this week's 3/10/30-year auctions and the late-July FOMC — sitting in the upper half of the band but still comfortably below the proven 5% ceiling. The regime that boxes it holds from both sides: a hawkish Fed on hold (band 3.50-3.75%, no cuts priced until 2027) anchors against a runaway break through 5%, while sticky inflation (the last PCE at 4.1% / core 3.4%) keeps it from rallying under 4% — exactly the boxed-in tape a range accrual is paid to harvest.
exEarnings.headline: With the 10Y back testing 4.5%, a proven 5% ceiling and a sticky-inflation floor box it — get paid an enhanced coupon to hold the range
exEarnings.thesis: The 10Y is back at ~4.49%, retesting the 4.5% area into Wednesday's June FOMC minutes and this week's 3/10/30-year auctions, held in a band by a hawkish Fed on hold (3.50-3.75%, no cuts priced until 2027) and inflation still sticky at the last PCE of 4.1% (core 3.4%). The TOP of the band is the harder call and the case is specific: 5% has been a proven ceiling — the 10Y peaked near 5.0% in October 2023 and has failed there on every test since, because at ~5% nominal (~2%+ real) duration is the cheapest in two decades and each approach draws heavy real-money buying — pensions and insurers matching liabilities, foreign reserve managers, and asset allocators rotating out of richly-valued equities. The BOTTOM (~4.00%) is held by sticky inflation — PCE at 4.1% — plus no cuts until 2027 to fuel a rally and an elevated term premium on heavy Treasury supply (this week alone brings 3/10/30-year auctions). So a 12-month 10Y range accrual pays an enhanced ~7.5% p.a. coupon (vs ~4.0% on cash) for each day the yield fixes inside 4.00-4.90%: you are paid to hold the boxed-in view, with the coupon forgone only on the days it breaks out. Capital is returned at par at maturity (issuer credit aside) — more than cash without an outright duration bet.

variant.us: Neither break holds: 5% is a proven buyer's ceiling and ~4% a sticky-inflation floor — monetise the range rather than bet on the break
variant.gap: The market pays up for the tail break in either direction and under-prices the 10Y simply staying boxed around 4.4-4.5%

exEarnings.changeMyMind: A decisive break of the box — a growth scare or sharp disinflation that pulls the 10Y below 4.00% (cuts brought forward), or a fiscal/supply/inflation shock that drives it through 5.00% — either zeros the accrual.

exEarnings.headline: A missile just hit an LNG tanker in Hormuz — own cheap Brent upside convexity while the structural tape is soft
exEarnings.thesis: The oil tape is genuinely two-sided and the cleanest expression is convexity, not direction. The STRUCTURAL picture is soft: Rory Johnston (Commodity Context, 2-3 Jul) reads Middle-East supply and Hormuz flows recovering faster than expected against weak Chinese demand, with prompt contango re-emerging for the first time since February — a 'dour', mini-glut tape around $72 Brent / $69 WTI, and OPEC+ having added a third +188K b/d step for August. Against that, the TAIL just re-fattened: an Iranian missile struck a Qatari LNG carrier in the Strait of Hormuz overnight, the first vessel strike since the truce, and TSCS ('The Paper Strait', 2-Jul) argues the market is underpricing the DURATION of a Hormuz disruption; Doomberg, structurally 'lower for longer', still carries a ~$140 one-year tail on Middle-East escalation. When flat price is soft but the tail is fat and oil vol is cheap — Moontower (2-Jul) notes oil vol concentrates in exactly these overnight/weekend windows — the honest trade is to buy defined-risk upside convexity via a 2-3 month Brent call spread, not to chase the spot glut in either direction.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: Tactical, event-driven commodity trade around a specific geopolitical shock — a short-dated defined-risk convexity position, not a standing desk commodity theme.

variant.street: Consensus is a soft, oversupplied summer crude tape drifting toward contango
variant.us: Flat price may be soft, but the Hormuz disruption tail is underpriced and oil vol is cheap — own upside convexity, not direction
variant.gap: The market prices the glut and discounts the chokepoint; a fresh tanker strike is exactly the tail it's underpricing

exEarnings.changeMyMind: A rapid Hormuz de-escalation — flows fully normalising with no follow-on strikes — that lets the structural glut and contango dominate and decays the premium; or a genuine sustained supply outage that turns this from a convexity trade into an outright long.

pillars.note: A ~$80/$92 call spread for ~1% premium pays a large multiple on a sustained escalation spike against a defined premium — strong long-tail convexity; the target is a soft, options-desk input.
Hard, dated overnight catalyst — a missile strike on an LNG tanker in the Strait of Hormuz, the first vessel hit since the truce, into a market that had priced the risk out.

The soft-tape consensus (Rory Johnston: contango, weak demand) is a confirming contra — we are not fighting flat price, we are buying the tail it discounts — but with no aligned bullish flow this is one leg, not two.

Quantified, sourced drivers (Brent ~$72, ~10% weekly drop, prompt contango, OPEC+ +188K, the Hormuz strike) and a computable variant — own convexity because flat price is soft while the disruption tail and oil vol are cheap.
Adjacent to the gold/geopolitics preservation lean, expressed as a tactical energy-convexity trade rather than a standing commodity theme.
Speculative length bled out on the reopening-hope selloff, so the crowd is offside a snap higher — mild fuel — but without a hard CoT read this is mixed, not extreme.

Brent basing ~$72 after a ~10% drop, below its recent range — a soft trend for flat price, but the trade is upside vol/convexity, so entry quality for the option is good even where the underlying trend is not.
